title: How I built a $250k/month Lovable agency (My Story)

segments.text: I make a ton of money every single
month building apps with Lovable. I'm
talking that my agency, Creme Digital,
does a quarter of a million dollars
every single month without fail building
apps with AI for clients all over the
world, ranging from startups,
enterprises, you name it, we built it.
Okay, this video isn't about me flexing.
I want to show you the exact steps, the
road map I took to get here. Because I
was once a kid in high school. I was
once somebody that sat in the back of
their class working on their computer
trying to make something of themselves
while the world was telling me to go go
to university, go take the safe route. I
knew that I wanted to do more. And so
this is a story of how I went from being
a broke high schooler, you know, a
skinny kid, somebody that didn't have a
lot of confidence, somebody that had no
idea what he wanted to do with his life,
and now I'm a 22-year-old. I make
millions of dollars. I have, you know,
my dream apartment here in an office in
a, you know, home studio. I sit here and
my life consists of building apps with
AI using Lovable. I get to travel the
world and I get to, you know, make
really cool products, really cool
startups, and meet really interesting
people. So, let me take you back to
where it all started in the back of my
high school classroom. You see, from a
very early age, I knew that I didn't
want to go to university. I just knew
that there was more to life than
building for somebody else, for sitting
in a cubicle, for a life that revolved
around working a set amount of hours. I
knew that I wanted to see the upside
that came with entrepreneurship. And I
knew that university wasn't the way to
do that. Now, for some of you watching,
that may not be the case. For for some
of you that may be questioning if this
is the path for you, maybe university
and college is the right route. But for
me, I knew that I wanted something more
and university wasn't the way to do it.
And so I started really young uh you
know when I was 13 I started building
different apps uh using no code tools
like bubble um and bubble for me was a
super pivotal moment. Um for those of
you who aren't familiar bubble is a no
code tool um that really is pretty
outdated now but at the time was a super
cool tool that I was able to leverage in
order to build apps being untenical. And
so I remember in high school building
multiple different apps. I built a
Airbnb for workspaces. So essentially
being able to connect people that had uh
workspaces or desks or you know like
conference rooms available with startups
or people looking to rent them out by
the hour. I built a Facebook marketplace
for my school and for the surrounding
school so kids could trade or you know
buy things off people in school to
create more of a safe ecosystem. Um and
there's so many other things I can name
that we that I built and it really you
know inspired this love for building. So
much so to the fact where when CO came
around, I had people starting to ask me
to help them build products. And one of
those companies was a taekwond do studio
in Toronto. And you know, when coa
happened, people were were freaking out.
They did not know what to do. And so I
came in here as and being the naive kid
I I was at the time, I thought, well,
I've been able to build apps in the past
using this tool called Bubble. So maybe
I can actually help other people do the
same. And so it was January or February
2020 when we took on our first client,
me and my co-founder who I met just
through the great vine of events and all
that stuff. Um, we were able to sign our
first big deal of around $50,000 at the
time. But before even we got there, we
were doing small hustles. We were trying
to just get a name for ourselves,
whether it was shooting real estate
videos, whether it was, you know, doing
social media marketing and Facebook ads
and all these various hustles. Um, but
the thing that I learned really early
and this is probably a really big lesson
for all you guys that are building right
now is that the people that do the best
are the people that are as niches
specific as possible. Okay, so you see
we went from bu doing all these various
business models, right? We tried, you
know, social media, photography, drop
shipping, e-commerce, all these
different things and we would offer it
all to our clients. But ultimately I
realized that building products using
these tools was our competitive
advantage. it was the thing that we
could focus on and become really good
at. And so that's what we did after we
found that first client. We realized
that this is a path that really, you
know, we could scale and we also just
enjoyed doing it. I mean, it was a lot
more fun than having to go to different
real estate shoots or going to do
various social media campaigns. I
actually enjoyed building products and
so after the first one, I was like,
well, what if we just kind of pivot the
entire company to only building products
with no code tools? And so that's
exactly what we did. Uh in 2021, we
rebranded our company, called it Creme
Digital instead of Creme Media, and we
went all in on building products for
startups and for enterprises. And I
believe the second we made that switch,
um things really took a turn because
what ends up happening is you start
focusing your efforts and you start
defining what you're good at and that
makes it a lot easier to go after
customers. And so I remember one of the
first things we did was just sign up for
um the partner programs that were
specific for building software. So we
signed up for all the tools that had
their own partner programs that would
essentially refer clients or refer
people that needed some expert, you
know, advice and agency help. And so
those programs basically got us to our
first, you know, $100,000, you know, I
would say I think it probably brought us
to around $600 $700,000 just in in in a
year just from those kind of programs.
Um, we ended up hiring a sales guy which
really helped us, you know, focus on the
actual fulfillment side of things while
we had somebody to kind of actually, you
know, close the deals because that was a
really timeconsuming part of the
process, especially back then when
you're just starting out. You really
have to prove yourself. So,
fast forwarding a bit, we we ran we
built this agency. And one of the things
that you know another big learning for
all you guys building right now is that
building an agency in particular means
that you need to have your own unique
angle on the market, right? And so for
ours, you know, we wanted to focus on
building apps. We wanted to focus on
building apps with no code and we wanted
to focus on building apps with no code
for startups. But there was one caveat
to this which is that no code was looked
at as a very old outdated kind of ugly
and clunky way of building software. And
to some extent that was true. You know
you had a lot of builders using these
tools and were building really crummy
products. But ultimately what we
realized is all these agencies all these
builders they were all just developers.
None of them had any design or product
experience. And so that's when we were
like well what if we actually focus on
that? What if we were able to charge
more because we're able to deliver a
higher quality product, but add in a
more, you know, worldclass design team,
a world a more polished end product to
make the price, you know, more valuable.
And so that's exactly what we did. Um,
and it that paid even more dividends.
The more we focused in on a niche, the
more the agency grew because we were
able to have a unique value prop in the
market. It was no longer competing
against a bunch of different
competitors. it was much easier to
define where we stood in the market
compared to our competitors. And
probably, you know, months after that,
we had our first $100,000 month, which
at the time, you have to you have to
remember, um, and maybe I didn't make
this clear, I was in high school this
entire time. And so, this is probably
around grade 12. And I remember, you
know, still having to go to class, but
seeing $50, $100,000 in Stripe, you
know, in my in my dashboard as I was
sitting in business class like being
lectured around how to run a business by
a teacher that's never done it before.
And so that's when I really realized I
was like, what what am I doing here? It
really reaffirmed this idea that like
university was not going to be the path
because I was just going to get more of
what I'm already seeing right now.
So ultimately the business continued to
do well and I convinced my parents and
myself frankly that it was the right
decision not to go to university and it
definitely took some convincing. Okay.
And and and one of the biggest things
that I had going for me was that the
business was working. The business was
able to show meaningful results based on
my efforts. And so I think that was a
promising signal for me. It was a
promising signal for my parents and
honestly a promising signal for the
team, the small team that we had at the
time to continue to build and so we
ended up kind of locking in and uh you
know I decided not to go and I spent the
rest of you know that first summer
focused on the business. It was the
first time where I was fully dedicated
to building something for for basically
my entire life. It was a really weird
feeling because, you know, for all of
you that that that go from, you know,
maybe building a side hustle and then
the side hustle becomes the main hustle,
it it goes from it just being what you
do on the weekends to basically your
entire life. It it it almost feels like
a simulation. Um I'm I'm not sure how
how relatable that is for a lot of you,
but for those of you who've been in that
situation, you kind of know. So, we end
up building this agency. It's doing
well, but we have one problem which is
that the no code space, the the space
that we are playing in, it just wasn't
big enough. Even though we are one of
the top agencies in the space, we just
were not able to break through, you
know, past that million mark. We were
able to hit the million dollar mark, but
we weren't really able to hit that $2
million mark or really even anywhere
close. And so I was starting to really
doubt the business. Um uh this is
probably you know we're we're we're like
2 3 years 3 4 years into the business at
this point. This basically is um from
you know probably 2021 2022 to 2024. So
basic basically like three-ish years and
um this was the end of last year and I
think the team myself included was kind
of tired. I mean obviously we had grown
the team a significant amount. We had
built a really good process. We have
really good clients. We had enterprise
clients even. But the reality was that
for whatever reason, we just could not
break through. Um, a lot of that is
probably learnings that, you know, I can
share um deeper in another video, but a
lot of that just had to do with the
market not being one that was growing
fast and one that was really able to,
you know, bring on the scale that we
were looking for. Because ultimately,
you're only as good as the boat that
you're riding. You're only as good as,
you know, the horse that you you pick.
So, you know, you should make sure that
the the market you're going into is one
that's actually growing because even if
you do nothing, even if all you do is
just provide a great experience and a
great service and a great product, you
will grow. Okay, that's the bare
minimum. And so it wasn't until January
2025, right? Things were looking pretty
bleak. Honestly, the agency was starting
to slow down. For whatever reason, I
think people were just starting to see
that these AI tools were coming along
and and people were starting to not want
to use no code tools very much anymore.
Um, on top of that, I think the team was
just kind of burnt out from stagnation.
It wasn't until I stumbled across this
tool. this tool which obviously is is
Lovable, but at the time it was not the
Lovable you know now. Um even though
this wasn't very long ago, Lovable was a
shell of the company it was today. Um or
it is today. Probably at the time doing
under $10 million a year. Um the design
looked nothing like it does today. The
product worked nothing like it does
today. It was basically an AI prompter
with a superb base connection. That was
basically it. Um, and I was playing with
this tool and I was very impressed
because there was a lot of different
note I mean a lot of different AI tools
in the market that I had I had tried but
they were all very gimmicky and this was
the first time where I actually tried a
tool and it was actually you know coming
up with an output that was reasonable
and I remember that first night I um I
started building a client project that
we had that usually would take us you
know months to build.
Um but I decided let's let's see let's
give this a shot. let's see what level
is capable of. And I would start
building it and you know at moments
where I was like okay well this is where
it's going to get stuck. It would fix
itself or I was like okay well it's
probably not going to be able to do this
and it didn't or it's probably not going
to be able to connect the back end and
it didn't. And I just kept running into
these obstacles that kept becoming
overcome. Um now now mind you I didn't
complete the entire app. Um if I kept
going I probably would have ran into
more milestones. But for everybody that
knows um vibe coding is kind of
bittersweet right? you can take the
product to about 80% of the way there
and you're riding this high. But usually
near the end of the last 20% is where
things can kind of get a little more
tricky. Obviously very overcome
overcomeable but takes a little bit more
you know debugging and maybe bring
things in the cursor etc. But at the
time I was just so impressed and so I
was like well now I get it. I get where
this is going to go now. Maybe this is a
bit early. Maybe, you know, I shouldn't
have been building client projects with
it, but I knew that this is going to be
the future and I want to get ahead of
the curve rather than be behind, even if
we're a month or two early because, as
you now know, this this this space is
moving incredibly fast. So, I decided
the next day to call an emergency
meeting with the team and we decided to
pivot the entire company. Okay, we never
built another bubble app again from that
point. And I got to say this, this was a
very monumental moment. This was a
moment that I think my team questioned.
I questioned and we had a lot of
headaches. It was not, you know, smooth
sailing. Lovable was just not really at
the point at the time to be building
these client grade products. But
luckily, we had a development team that
was already super sophisticated and was
able to, you know, leverage their
technical skills to move it to Cursor or
move it to other tools when we needed to
while still keeping Lovable as our base.
Now, Lovable at the time, as I said, was
not the company it is today. When they
saw people like us actually trying to
build an agency on top of Lovable, they
reached out and they were like, "How can
we help you?" And so, that's when we
kind of started building this agency
partnership page that we'll throw up
here, lovable.dev/partners.
And basically, that is where we kind of
got our start. That is when the Lovable
ecosystem finally started. And it was
like probably a month after we started
building we we we kind of had that
initial partnership with Loveable and we
slowly worked with them on improving the
product for themselves but also for our
customers and we made it apparent to
like where are the things that we need
in order to be able to serve clients and
they were incredibly responsive and in
incredibly eager to help that process
and within about you know a month or two
after that lovable was in you know a
very different place at this point we're
probably coming into March April Okay.
And Lovable is starting to really
evolve. Okay. Lovable is starting to
become a much more fleshed out product.
You know, it's growing super fast. And
now all of a sudden, we are this agency
that, you know, started when there was
probably two or three agencies. And now
we're like the top agency in a space
that is starting to get really
competitive and really crowded. But
because we moved early and because we
built that relationship early, we had
that first mover's advantage. Things
were going well. Um I went I posted
about this this process on Twitter and
went viral multiple times and that only
exploited the growth of both myself the
agency lovable etc. I think when people
realize that you can make serious money
on lovable the perspective of lovable
changed. It changed from this like
gimmicky tool that people are building
apps with for fun and you know maybe
launching them to like no startups real
startups that are getting venture funded
are using lovable to build their MVP
products and they're actually looking
good and they're usable and they're
scalable and all these things. It was a
complete shift that ended up happening
over those 2 months thanks to lovable
improving their product, thanks to
agencies like ourselves that were
building and thanks to content creators
that were making people aware of this of
this impact. Things were going great.
Obviously, we are running into our
obstacles here and there, but things
really took a turn. Uh, in about I would
have to say June. Okay, June was the
first time I had met Anton in New York.
Anton is the CEO of Loveable and and we
wanted to kind of start talking about
what it meant to like deepen our
partnership. And what we came up with
was a few things. The first thing is
that we wanted to give gold agencies, us
being the first one, even more support.
So, how can we how can lovable and and
agencies have an even more direct
relationship in working with clients,
especially when it came to enterprise
clients. So, that was the first thing.
The other thing was on the content
strategy side of things. He really
wanted us as agency owners and as you
know, builders ourselves to be involved
in creating content. So we partnered on
different webinars and content things
that we can able that enabled other
users to see how to build on lovable. Um
and ultimately this resulted in a trip
to Sweden. Um this trip will be forever
ingrained in my brain because not only
was it just a legendary trip for those

the place. But the best way I can
describe it is going to Facebook offices
in 200
for whenever they launch like the early

the entire country is riding for this
this this uh the startup. Lovable is
like the thing in Sweden. And we went to

so, not only was, you know, the
experience of being in the office with
Lovable, but even just exploring the
city through the lens of Lovable was
such an interesting and different
experience. People would come up to us
and be like, you know, are you with the

some builders from all over Stockholm,
Sweden, and we hosted our own panel with

really successful and a really, you
know, amazing thing that we got to be a
part of. So many just amazing
experiences, but ultimately it came with
one big moment, which is that we finally
solidified this big gold partnership.
This partnership that would that would
solidify us as the number one lovable
agency as the first and only gold agency
at the time and just a really big
milestone for us and Lovable as we
continue this next chapter. There's a
lot more I can get into. If you guys

how I went from a 17-year-old um that

that, you know, step by step different,
you know, this is this is over 6 years
now. Okay, I'm the product of a six
years of hard work. And I want that to
be abundantly clear. This is not
something that you can just snap your

But with the same hard work, with the
